summary: Jeff Atwood explores the history and current state of computer chess, examining how brute-force approaches dominated early computer chess but strategic AI programs are now emerging as viable competitors. He compares his personal PC's chess-playing capability (4.45 million positions/second) to Deep Blue's 200 million, noting that desktop hardware lags 15 years behind specialized chess computers. However, the more significant development is that Type B programs like Deep Fritz can match Deep Blue's performance through smarter move filtering rather than raw computation. Atwood disputes claims that computers have definitively surpassed humans, citing evidence that top grandmasters have maintained drawn matches against the best programs, suggesting we've reached an equilibrium point.
key_points: Brute force (Type A) dominated computer chess history because computers struggle with the concept of 'good' positions, while strategic AI (Type B) programs are finally becoming competitive
Desktop PCs in 2006 were over 15 years behind Deep Blue's specialized hardware in raw chess calculation speed
Deep Fritz demonstrates that intelligent move filtering can match brute force computation - it achieves similar depth despite vastly inferior positions-per-second
Despite claims of computer supremacy, all post-1997 matches between world champions and top chess programs resulted in ties
The top 200 human players have held their ground against improving computers, suggesting a dynamic equilibrium rather than inevitable computer dominance
money_quotes: Chess remains the most visible and public benchmark of the relentless increase in computer speed over the last 50 years.
As it turns out, computers have a hard time with the concept of 'good.'
The fastest desktop PCs are more than 15 years behind Deep Blue in computer chess.
Despite its vastly inferior brute force, the Deep Blitz machine could already be a match for Deep Blue because of improvements in chess software.

Obviously, Kasparov did not evaluate 200 million chess positions per second when he defeated Deep Blue in game 1 of the 1997 match, thus the 200 million positions per second number is not a requirement to play chess at the world championship level.
The top computers have caught up with the top grandmasters, and right now I'm not convinced that the computers will zoom past the grandmasters.
The problem space of chess is so astonishingly large that incremental increases in hardware speed and algorithms are unlikely to result in meaningful gains from here on out.
Computer chess programs may have long ago crushed the rest of us in their inevitable Moore's Law march, but the best 200 chess players in the world are still holding their ground.
key_insight: Smart algorithms that filter for quality moves may be more valuable than raw computational brute force, and human expertise at the highest levels may prove surprisingly resilient against machine optimization.
